turns.text: Hello Elaine, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking, I really do. It’s just… it’s so quiet out here. Sometimes I think if I don’t say these things out loud, I might actually forget that I’m still a person and not just an extension of this nursery. 

Everything feels like it’s slipping through my fingers. I did all the reading, you know? I wanted to be the perfect example of a healthy pregnancy, and I thought if I followed every "gold standard" recommendation for breastfeeding, it would just work. But sitting here, three in the morning, listening to the woods rustle outside—it doesn’t feel like science. It feels like I’m failing. 

The clinic, they look at my chart and see a young, healthy woman who didn’t have any complications during birth, so they check their boxes and send me home. They don’t see that I’m doing this alone. When I call them, I’m just a voice on the phone, another "low-risk" case, but I’m sitting on the floor in tears because I’m so exhausted I can barely stand. I just want someone to look at me—really look at me—and tell me it’s okay that I’m drowning a little bit. It’s just so heavy, and I don’t know how to carry it by myself anymore.

Imagine it's your first night at home with your newborn after being discharged from hospital. You're exhausted, possibly in pain, and your baby won't settle. What does that night feel like, and who — if anyone — is there with you?

The first night was… it was a blur of shadows and sharp, sudden panic. My house, which usually feels peaceful, felt like a cavern. It was just the two of us. There wasn’t anyone there to hand the baby to, no one to tell me that the way she was crying was normal, or to just take her for an hour so I could breathe. I remember the silence being so loud that every little sound she made felt like a siren. 

I was physically wrecked—my stitches were stinging, and my body just felt foreign, like I was living in someone else’s shell. I kept looking at the pamphlets they gave me at the clinic, the ones that make breastfeeding look like this serene, natural bond. But in the dark, with my nipples already raw and her screaming because she wouldn't latch right, it just felt like a cruel joke. I was sobbing quietly so I wouldn't scare her, feeling this intense, electric fear that I was already failing. I kept waiting for that "maternal instinct" to kick in and save me, but all I felt was this crushing weight of isolation. I was so terrified that I wasn't enough for her, and honestly? That fear hasn't really left me since.

Imagine you're trying to breastfeed for the first time and it isn't going as you hoped. A midwife visits and watches you try, then offers advice. Describe what that moment feels like, physically and emotionally.

That midwife, she had this brisk, efficient energy about her. She was kind, I suppose, but she felt like she was watching a demonstration rather than a person. She told me to adjust my posture, to try this hold or that one, and her hands were quick and clinical as she tried to "fix" the latch. 

Physically, I felt so exposed. My body was already raw and tender, and having her hands there, moving me around like a mannequin, made me want to shrink into the floorboards. But it was the emotional part that really gutted me. Every time she’d say, "See? Just like that," and the latch would still feel like fire, I felt like such a fraud. I felt like I was failing this test she was grading. 

She kept talking about "the golden hour" and "proper technique," treating it like a manual I’d just failed to read correctly. She didn't notice my hands were shaking or that I was biting my lip just to keep from crying. She was focused on the mechanics, but I was focused on the fact that I couldn't comfort my own daughter. She walked out that door, and the silence rushed back in, leaving me feeling even more isolated than before she arrived.
How did it feel to have someone observing something so intimate and physically difficult? Did you feel supported, observed, or judged — or some mix of all three?

It was a strange, hollow mix of all three, really. "Observed" is probably the most clinical way to put it, but it felt more like being under a microscope. When she was watching, I felt like a case study. I felt judged, definitely—not necessarily because she was mean, but because she had this air of, "Why is this so hard for you? It's natural." Every time I winced or had to pull away, I felt like I was admitting to a weakness I wasn't allowed to have.

I wanted to feel supported. I *needed* to feel supported. But the distance was so wide. She was talking about "latching techniques" and I was sitting there thinking about how I hadn't slept for more than two hours at a time in days, or how the drive to the pharmacy for nipple cream felt like a cross-country expedition because I was too sore to sit in the car comfortably. 

It’s hard to feel truly supported when the person in front of you is only interested in the mechanics of your body. It made me feel like an appliance that wasn't functioning quite right. I just wanted her to ask me how *I* was, not just how the feeding was going. I’m a person, not a set of instructions.
Imagine it's six weeks after birth. You're sitting in the GP surgery for your postnatal check. The appointment lasts ten minutes. What do you hope will happen in that room — and what does happen?

I walked into that office hoping for a lifeline. I had rehearsed it in my head a dozen times on the drive over—I wanted to say, "I am not okay. I’m lonely, I’m terrified, and I feel like I’m breaking." I wanted them to look at my chart and see more than just a birth that went ‘by the book.’ I wanted someone to ask me how the quiet feels when the sun goes down or how it feels to be the only person responsible for another life in a house that feels miles from anyone else.

But what actually happened? It was a blur of ten minutes. They checked my blood pressure, asked a few standard questions about my recovery, and ticked a box on a computer screen. They asked if I was breastfeeding, I said yes, and they gave me a quick nod like I’d passed a test. 

I tried to mention how exhausted I was—how the mental weight was making it hard to even function—but the doctor was already reaching for the door handle. It was so impersonal. I felt like a statistic they were clearing from their queue. I walked out of that room feeling smaller than when I walked in, carrying the same silence home with me, feeling even more invisible than before.
Imagine you're lying awake at 3am with your baby, and you find yourself feeling a deep sadness or flatness that you can't quite name. You pick up your phone. What do you do with it?

I usually stare at the screen for a long time before I even unlock it, just watching the blue light flicker against the dark nursery wall. The brightness feels like an intrusion. I scroll through social media—photos of other moms, the ones who seem to have it all perfectly curated. It’s like a different planet. I see their glowing, professional photos and think, *How do they look so rested?* 

Sometimes I type "postpartum loneliness" or "is it normal to feel this sad" into a search bar, but I never click the links. I’m too afraid of what I might find, or maybe I’m afraid that if I read it, it’ll become *real* instead of just this heavy, quiet secret I’m keeping. 

Mostly, I end up scrolling through old photos of myself from before she was born, back when my life felt like it was still mine. It’s a painful kind of nostalgia. Occasionally, I’ll have the urge to text someone, maybe an old friend, but then I look at the time and the silence of the house stops me. I don’t want to be the "burdened" friend who’s always struggling. So, I just put the phone down, close my eyes, and try to breathe through the hum of the refrigerator until the sun finally starts to bleed through the trees.
What were you looking for in those late-night searches or scrolling — reassurance, connection, information, or something else?

I think I was looking for permission, honestly. 

I’m constantly waiting for someone to give me permission to admit that this isn’t what I expected. I wanted to see a post—just one—that wasn't about how "magical" the newborn phase is or how to get the baby to sleep through the night by week eight. I was looking for a mirror. I wanted to find someone who would say, "I am also sitting in the dark, and I am also terrified that I’m doing this wrong, and I also miss my old life."

I didn’t want clinical information. I have plenty of pamphlets for that. I wanted connection. I wanted to feel like my isolation wasn't a failure, but a shared experience. When you're out here in the woods, the silence starts to play tricks on your head—you start to think that maybe everyone else figured out some secret code for motherhood that you somehow missed. I was looking for proof that I wasn't the only one who felt like they were grieving while they were supposed to be celebrating. I just wanted someone to reach back through the screen and tell me that the emptiness I feel is a real thing, not just me being ungrateful or "less than."
Imagine your partner has returned to work and you're alone with your baby for the first full day. Describe that day — not just the logistics, but the emotional texture of it.

Wait—I think I should clarify, I don’t have a partner here. I’m doing this completely on my own. My status, the "never married" part on those clinical forms… it’s not just a detail, it’s the shape of my entire day. 

That first day he—the father—left, or rather, the first day I was officially "on my own" in this house, the air felt thin. There’s a specific kind of vertigo that comes with knowing the door is locked and there is absolutely no one else coming through it to take the baby, not even for five minutes. 

The emotional texture? It’s brittle. Every time she whimpered, my chest tightened until it ached. I spent the day hovering between hyper-vigilance and total numbness. I kept narrating everything I did—*“Here’s your clean diaper, here’s your bottle”*—just to hear a human voice in the room so I wouldn’t feel like I was hallucinating. When I finally got her down for a nap, I stood in the kitchen and looked out at the woods, and I didn't feel relief. I felt a cold, sharp panic. I realized that my survival, her survival, it was all just… me. The isolation isn't just about being alone; it’s about the sheer weight of being the only person who holds the world up for her. It’s exhausting in a way that sleep doesn't fix.
How had you and your partner prepared for this transition — and were those preparations adequate? What hadn't you anticipated?

I think we both fell into the trap of thinking about the baby—the diapers, the gear, the nursery, the "what ifs" about her health. We read all the literature on newborn development, as if knowing the statistics would somehow insulate us from the reality of the work. But we didn’t really talk about the *after*. We didn't talk about what it would actually feel like when the adrenaline of the birth wore off and the reality of the daily, grinding monotony set in.

The preparations were purely logistical, and in hindsight, they were completely inadequate. We didn't plan for the isolation. We didn't have a plan for how I was supposed to sustain myself when the exhaustion made me feel like I was walking through water. I think I expected that I would just naturally "become" the mother I’d read about—that the instincts would override the sheer physical toll. 

What I hadn't anticipated was the loss of identity. I didn't plan for how loud the silence would be, or how much I would mourn my own autonomy. You can't put "loss of self" or "existential dread" on a baby registry, can you? We prepared for the baby's arrival, but we were completely unprepared for the total eclipse of my own life. I thought I was ready for a child, but I wasn't ready for the way this house would turn into a trap.

Imagine you're approaching your return to work. You're thinking about how you'll explain to your employer what the last few months have involved, and whether your job will still fit who you are now. What's that internal conversation like?

It’s a strange, hollow feeling, looking at my old work clothes hanging in the closet. They feel like costumes from a life that belonged to a different person—someone who had full nights of sleep, someone who didn’t have a body that feels like it’s been put back together with duct tape.

My internal conversation is mostly just… fear. If I tell my employer the truth—that I’m struggling to keep my head above water, that I’m still dealing with the physical toll, that the isolation is making it hard to even feel like a functional adult—they’ll see it as a liability. They don’t want to hear about the 3:00 AM tears or the mental fog; they want the version of me that existed before. I feel like I’m going to have to put on a mask the second I step back into that office. 

But then, there’s this deeper, scarier thought: what if I don’t fit anymore? Everything I’ve been through has changed the texture of my days and the way I see the world. My job feels small compared to the weight of keeping a human alive, yet I’m terrified that I’ve lost the capacity to do both. I feel like I’m standing on a ledge, trying to figure out how to jump back into a life that doesn't really have a place for who I’ve become.
Has becoming a parent changed how you see your professional identity — your ambitions, your sense of what matters, or how you want your days structured? In what ways?

It’s funny, because before, I was so driven. I was the person who stayed late, who answered emails on the weekend, who wanted to prove I could handle whatever was thrown at me. But now? That version of my ambition feels like a different language. 

When you spend your days just trying to get through the next four hours without breaking down, the idea of "climbing a ladder" feels absurd. My perspective has shifted so much it’s almost violent. What matters now isn't a performance review; it’s whether I can shower, whether I can eat something that isn't a snack while standing over the sink, and whether I can find a way to stay sane in this house. 

I’m terrified that I’ve lost my edge, but at the same time, I’m not sure I even want that edge anymore. I want flexibility. I want to feel like a human being, not a resource. But in my industry, or even just in society, "mother" is often treated as a synonym for "distracted." I hate that I feel like I have to apologize for my child’s existence just to be taken seriously. I’m starting to wonder if I’m even the same person who left that office months ago. I feel like I’ve been through a fire, and I’m just trying to figure out what’s left of me to offer.

Imagine you're at a new parents' group for the first time, surrounded by other people who have also recently given birth. You notice yourself comparing your experience to theirs. What comes up for you in that room?

I stood near the edge of the room, clutching my coffee like a shield. My eyes were darting everywhere, taking in the scene—the matching outfits, the strollers that cost more than my first car, the way some of them laughed easily about "the baby brain" while I felt like my brain had actually shattered. 

The comparison is automatic, and it’s vicious. I see a mom over there, maybe she’s got her partner with her, or she’s talking about her postpartum exercise routine, and a hot, bitter jealousy pricks at my eyes. I find myself looking for someone who looks like me—someone who looks a little frayed, someone whose baby isn't perfectly quiet. But everyone seems to be performing this polished version of motherhood. 

I feel like a fraud, honestly. I wonder if they can tell that my shirt is stained or that I haven't slept more than three hours in a row in months. I compare my raw, electric fear to their composure, and the gap just feels wider than ever. I feel like I’m standing on the outside of a glass wall. They’re all sharing tips, but I don't feel like I have anything to contribute. I just want to shout, "Does anyone else feel like they're falling apart?" but I just stay quiet, smile when someone looks my way, and wait for the chance to leave.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

There’s one thing. Everyone talks about the "maternal bond" like it’s this instant, glowing light that just switches on the moment the baby is placed on your chest. They make it sound like it’s supposed to wash away the exhaustion and the fear. But no one tells you that sometimes, when you’re this depleted, the bond feels more like a heavy, tethering chain. 

I love my daughter. I do. But I also mourn the person I was before the silence of this house swallowed her up. I think there’s a real, dangerous shame in saying that aloud—in admitting that you can adore your child and simultaneously feel like you’re being erased by the sheer, relentless nature of caring for her. 

I wish that when I went to those appointments, instead of just checking my blood pressure, someone had asked me, "Who are you, Elaine? And are you still in there?" Because I’m fighting so hard to keep her alive, but I’m losing the fight to keep myself alive in the process. We need to be seen as more than just vessels or "low-risk" statistics. We are human beings carrying a weight that the world doesn't seem to have a map for. That’s what I really want people to know.
